item.title: CVE-2026-0257 Exploitation, Apache HTTP/2 DoS, Nuxt and UniFi Flaws
item.description: This digest is dominated by newly reported and actively exploited vulnerabilities affecting internet-facing infrastructure, web application frameworks, network appliances, and VPN access systems. The highest operational concern is Palo Alto Networks’ report of active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257...
item.content:encoded:
This digest is dominated by newly reported and actively exploited vulnerabilities affecting internet-facing infrastructure, web application frameworks, network appliances, and VPN access systems. The highest operational concern is Palo Alto Networks’ report of active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257 in PAN-OS GlobalProtect, alongside multiple pre-authentication flaws that could affect exposed web and management surfaces. Palo Alto Networks reported active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257, an authentication bypass vulnerability affecting the portal and gateway components of PAN-OS GlobalProtect. The company said an unknown threat actor used the flaw to gain unauthorized access to GlobalProtect portals, with initial observed activity dating to May 17, 2026, and limited exploitation resulting in some VPN sessions being established. The vulnerability was added to CISA’s Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, reflecting confirmed exploitation in the wild. Palo Alto Networks stated that it had not identified post-access behavior or lateral movement in the observed cases, and that only a small portion of probed devices successfully established VPN connections. Apache HTTP Server was also highlighted for CVE-2026-49975, a denial-of-service vulnerability in the modhttp2 module affecting versions 2.4.17 through 2.4.67 when HTTP/2 is enabled. The reported attack combines an HPACK compression bomb with an HTTP/2 flow-control hold, causing rapid memory growth while keeping allocations alive; Apache HTTP Server 2.4.68 was released on June 8, 2026, with a fix for this and other vulnerabilities. Nuxt applications are affected by CVE-2026-53721, a route-rule middleware bypass caused by a case-sensitivity mismatch between vue-router and Nuxt’s routeRules matcher. The issue can be triggered before authentication and without user interaction by altering URL path casing, such as requesting /Admin instead of /admin, and affects Nuxt 3.11.0 through versions before 3.21.7 and Nuxt 4.0.0 through versions before 4.4.7. Ubiquiti UniFi OS was reported vulnerable to CVE-2026-47368, a path traversal issue caused by inadequate validation of user-supplied input during file path construction. The vulnerability has a CVSS v3.1 score of 8.6, requires no authentication or user interaction, and can allow attackers with network access to read arbitrary files, including configuration files, logs, and sensitive application data. Affected UniFi products include UniFi OS Server versions before 5.1.15, Express line versions before 4.0.15, and UDM family versions before 5.1.15. Ubiquiti published fixed versions in Security Advisory Bulletin 065, making this another example of exposure risk concentrated around management interfaces and edge-adjacent infrastructure. Group-IB disclosed fraudulent campaigns targeting users across the Middle East and North Africa through Facebook accounts impersonating politicians, public figures, trusted organizations, and telecom providers. The campaigns were attributed to Sniper Dz, a phishing-as-a-service platform that was taken down in an INTERPOL-led operation last month. Victims were lured with fake offers such as free mobile internet packages, financial compensation, and government subsidy programs, then redirected through intermediary websites to phishing and traffic monetization infrastructure. The scam pages abused browser notification permissions using a VAPID public key, with Group-IB observing the same key across campaigns impersonating Algerian telecom providers and investment scams across multiple regions. The operators also used back-button hijacking, tab-under redirections, and a traffic distribution system to send victims toward premium-rate call scams, premium SMS subscription fraud, and investment scams. Separately, Constella described how OSINT teams use identity intelligence to correlate emails, usernames, phone numbers, credentials, social accounts, breach data, infrastructure, and public records to accelerate attribution, exposure analysis, relationship mapping, and investigative triage.

Palo Alto Warns of Active Exploitation of PAN-OS GlobalProtect VPN Flaw — Must Read

Palo Alto Networks says CVE-2026-0257, an authentication bypass flaw in PAN-OS GlobalProtect portals and gateways, is being actively exploited in limited attacks to establish unauthorized VPN sessions. Customers should check GlobalProtect logs for the cited indicators and mitigate the flaw, which CISA has added to its KEV catalog.

  • Palo Alto Networks observed active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257 by an unknown threat actor to gain unauthorized access to GlobalProtect portals.
  • CVE-2026-0257 is an authentication bypass flaw affecting the portal and gateway components of PAN-OS software that could allow attackers to set up VPN connections.
  • Palo Alto Networks said the vulnerability has been exploited in limited attacks, with initial activity observed on May 17, 2026.
  • Palo Alto Networks said no post-access behavior or lateral movement has been identified, and only a small portion of probed devices established VPN sessions.
  • CISA added CVE-2026-0257 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog and ordered Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to mitigate the flaw by June 1, 2026.

Emerging Threat: (CVE-2026-49975) Apache HTTP Server Denial of Service via HTTP/2 Memory Exhaustion — Should Read

CVE-2026-49975 is a denial-of-service flaw in Apache HTTP Server’s mod_http2 that can let unauthenticated attackers exhaust memory on HTTP/2-enabled servers using crafted requests. Organizations should upgrade to Apache 2.4.68 or later, verify downstream patches, and mitigate by limiting or disabling HTTP/2 where patching is not yet possible.

  • CVE-2026-49975 is a memory exhaustion vulnerability in Apache HTTP Server’s mod_http2 module that allows a remote attacker to cause denial of service through maliciously crafted HTTP/2 requests.
  • The flaw affects Apache HTTP Server versions 2.4.17 through 2.4.67, typically on internet-facing web servers or reverse proxies with HTTP/2 enabled.
  • The attack technique combines an HPACK compression bomb with an HTTP/2 flow-control hold to drive rapid memory growth and keep allocations alive.
  • Apache Software Foundation released Apache HTTP Server 2.4.68 on June 8, 2026, to fix CVE-2026-49975 and other vulnerabilities.
  • Recommended actions include inventorying HTTP/2-enabled Apache httpd and nginx servers, limiting HTTP/2 streams and request headers, monitoring for abnormal memory growth, and disabling HTTP/2 on exposed servers that cannot be patched promptly.

Emerging Threat: (CVE-2026-47368) UniFi OS Information Disclosure via Path Traversal — Should Read

CVE-2026-47368 is a high-severity, unauthenticated path traversal flaw in UniFi OS that can expose sensitive files on affected Ubiquiti devices. Organizations should identify all UniFi appliances, restrict public management access, and update to the fixed versions listed in Ubiquiti’s advisory.

  • CVE-2026-47368 is a path traversal vulnerability in Ubiquiti’s UniFi OS caused by inadequate validation of user-supplied input when constructing file paths.
  • The vulnerability has a CVSS v3.1 base score of 8.6, requires no authentication or user interaction, and can be exploited by an attacker with network access to a vulnerable device.
  • Successful exploitation allows an attacker to read arbitrary files on the device, including configuration files, logs, and other sensitive application data.
  • Affected products include UniFi OS Server versions before 5.1.15, the Express line before 4.0.15, and the UDM family before 5.1.15.
  • Ubiquiti has published fixed versions in Security Advisory Bulletin 065, and defenders are advised to inventory UniFi OS appliances, restrict management access, block public exposure of management ports, monitor logs, and prioritize internet-facing devices for patching.

Emerging Threat: (CVE-2026-53721) Nuxt Route-Rule Middleware Bypass via Case-Sensitivity Mismatch — Should Read

Nuxt CVE-2026-53721 lets attackers bypass route-rule middleware by changing the case of a protected path, potentially skipping access controls or other path-level protections. Affected Nuxt 3.x and 4.x applications should upgrade to 3.21.7 or 4.4.7 or later and avoid relying on routeRules alone for security boundaries.

  • CVE-2026-53721 is a Nuxt route-rule middleware bypass caused by a case-sensitivity mismatch between vue-router and Nuxt’s routeRules matcher.
  • An attacker can exploit the issue before authentication and without user interaction by sending HTTP requests with altered path casing, such as using /Admin instead of /admin.
  • The vulnerability affects Nuxt versions 3.11.0 through versions before 3.21.7 and Nuxt versions 4.0.0 through versions before 4.4.7.
  • Nuxt maintainers fixed the issue in versions 3.21.7 and 4.4.7, and organizations should upgrade affected applications to those versions or later.
  • Defenders should inventory internet-facing Nuxt applications, verify exact running versions, normalize request path casing where appropriate, and monitor sensitive paths for unusual casing.

Sniper Dz Scams Target MENA Users via Fake Facebook Offers and Browser Alerts — Should Read

Group-IB says Sniper Dz campaigns targeted MENA users with fake Facebook offers impersonating trusted figures and telecom brands, funneling victims through link-aggregation pages into phishing and traffic-monetization infrastructure. The scams abused browser notifications, back-button hijacking, tab-under redirects, premium SMS/call fraud, and investment scams rather than relying on traditional malware.

  • Group-IB disclosed fraudulent campaigns targeting users across the Middle East and North Africa through Facebook accounts impersonating politicians, public figures, trusted organizations, and telecom providers.
  • The campaigns are attributed to Sniper Dz, a phishing-as-a-service platform that was taken down in an INTERPOL-led operation last month.
  • Victims were lured with fake offers such as free mobile internet packages, financial compensation, and government subsidy programs, then redirected through intermediary websites to phishing and traffic monetization infrastructure.
  • The scam pages abused browser notification permissions using a VAPID public key, and Group-IB observed the same key across campaigns impersonating Algerian telecom providers and investment scams in multiple regions.
  • The operators also used back-button hijacking, tab-under redirections, and a traffic distribution system to route victims to premium-rate call scams, premium SMS subscription fraud, and investment scams.

How OSINT Teams Use Identity Intelligence to Accelerate Investigations — Optional

OSINT investigations increasingly struggle with attribution, not data collection, because investigators must connect scattered emails, usernames, accounts, and exposure records to real identities. The article argues that Identity Intelligence speeds investigations by correlating these fragments into clearer identity, relationship, and risk context.

  • OSINT investigators can access large amounts of information from sources such as social media platforms, public records, data breaches, forums, news sources, technical infrastructure, and online marketplaces.
  • The article states that modern investigations often stall because investigators must determine who is behind fragments such as an email address, username, phone number, credential, or social media account.
  • Identity Intelligence focuses on understanding how identities connect across datasets, sources, and environments to help investigators determine ownership, relationships, exposure, and evolving risk.
  • Identity Intelligence can enrich investigations with identity correlations, historical exposure, associated identifiers, relationship mapping, and contextual risk indicators.
  • Constella says its identity-centric intelligence helps investigators accelerate investigations, improve attribution, reduce false positives, identify hidden relationships, and generate stronger intelligence outcomes.

item.title: FBI Disrupts Outsider Enterprise AI-Powered Phishing-as-a-Service Operation
item.description: This digest window is dominated by a major law enforcement disruption of a large-scale phishing-as-a-service ecosystem attributed to a Chinese operation known as Outsider Enterprise. Reporting indicates the service industrialized credential and payment-card theft through thousands of phishing...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by a major law enforcement disruption of a large-scale phishing-as-a-service ecosystem attributed to a Chinese operation known as Outsider Enterprise. Reporting indicates the service industrialized credential and payment-card theft through thousands of phishing sites, more than a million fraudulent URLs, SMS delivery at scale, and AI-enabled tooling. The case reflects continued convergence between cybercrime infrastructure, mobile messaging abuse, automation, and hosted e-commerce-style service models. It also highlights growing collaboration between law enforcement, cloud and internet infrastructure providers, telecommunications carriers, and security researchers in disrupting fraud operations before messages and phishing links reach end users. The FBI, working with Google and Black Lotus Labs, dismantled infrastructure tied to Outsider Enterprise, a Chinese phishing-as-a-service operation accused of running thousands of fraudulent websites designed to steal credit card data and passwords. Google linked the operation to approximately 9,000 fake websites and more than one million fraudulent URLs, illustrating the scale at which the group generated and distributed phishing infrastructure. Authorities believe campaigns connected to Outsider Enterprise stole more than 3.8 million credit card records and caused an estimated $1.9 billion in losses. During the disruption, the FBI and partners seized multiple administration servers, a Shopify storefront, a testing account, roughly $100,000 in USDT, and a Telegram bot associated with the operation. The seized assets point to a mature service model spanning web hosting, testing workflows, payment infrastructure, and operator communications. Google also filed a civil lawsuit targeting the operation’s infrastructure and said it is coordinating with AT&T, T-Mobile, and Verizon to block fraudulent messages before they reach subscribers. According to Google, Outsider Enterprise sent 2.5 million SMS messages to Android users over a two-week period in May, and Android users flagged 55,000 of those messages as fraudulent. The reported SMS volume underscores the role of mobile delivery channels in modern phishing operations and the operational emphasis on rapid, high-volume lure distribution. The reporting characterizes Outsider Enterprise as AI-powered, reflecting the broader trend of fraud services adopting automation and generative tooling to scale phishing content, infrastructure, and campaign management. While the article focuses on the takedown rather than technical indicators, the scope of domains, URLs, SMS traffic, and stolen payment records makes the operation one of the more significant recent examples of phishing-as-a-service disruption.

item.title: InformData-Impersonation Phishing Passed DMARC via Authorized SendGrid Infrastructure
item.description: This digest covers a phishing case in which email authentication controls validated the sending path but did not establish message legitimacy. The reported activity highlights a common detection challenge for security teams: attacker abuse or misconfiguration of authorized third-party email...
item.content:encoded: This digest covers a phishing case in which email authentication controls validated the sending path but did not establish message legitimacy. The reported activity highlights a common detection challenge for security teams: attacker abuse or misconfiguration of authorized third-party email infrastructure can allow malicious messages to pass SPF, DKIM, and DMARC checks even under strict enforcement. IRONSCALES reported an employment verification-themed phishing email impersonating InformData that was sent through SendGrid infrastructure and passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC checks, including a DMARC policy set to REJECT. The message was associated with SendGrid host wfbtrqkw.outbound-mail.sendgrid.net and IP address 159[.]183[.]84[.]25, which the article states were configured as authorized sending infrastructure for international.informdata[.]com. The phishing lure targeted a sports data technology company and was flagged in June 2026 after a colleague at the organization reported the email. According to IRONSCALES, one mailbox was quarantined. The message used an employment verification pretext while redirecting replies and user interaction away from the apparent sender’s legitimate domain. The email’s Reply-To header directed responses to support@verifying[.]you, while the call-to-action linked to verify.zippedscript[.]com; both were described by IRONSCALES as attacker-controlled domains. This combination created a mismatch between the authenticated sending domain and the destinations used for follow-up interaction, allowing the message to appear technically authenticated while steering the recipient into attacker-controlled communication paths. The case illustrates the distinction between domain authentication and message intent. As described in the report, DMARC validated that the message was sent via infrastructure authorized for the domain, but it did not determine whether the email content, Reply-To destination, or embedded link represented a legitimate business process. For defenders and threat hunters, the incident is notable because the malicious indicators were not authentication failures, but rather the use of authorized infrastructure alongside suspicious reply routing and external verification domains.

item.title: Outsider Enterprise Takedown and DMARC-Passing Employment Verification Phishing
item.description: This 24-hour digest window is dominated by phishing infrastructure and delivery abuse, spanning both large-scale phishing-as-a-service operations and targeted business-themed credential or data-harvesting lures. The reporting highlights how attackers are combining trusted platforms, authenticated...
item.content:encoded: This 24-hour digest window is dominated by phishing infrastructure and delivery abuse, spanning both large-scale phishing-as-a-service operations and targeted business-themed credential or data-harvesting lures. The reporting highlights how attackers are combining trusted platforms, authenticated sending paths, per-recipient tracking, and high-volume messaging to improve delivery and attribution of victim engagement. The FBI, working with Google and Black Lotus Labs, disrupted a Chinese phishing-as-a-service operation tracked as Outsider Enterprise. Google linked the operation to approximately 9,000 fake websites and more than one million fraudulent URLs used to steal credit card data and passwords. Authorities believe campaigns associated with the service stole more than 3.8 million credit card records and caused an estimated $1.9 billion in losses. The takedown reportedly included the seizure of multiple administration servers, a Shopify storefront, a testing account, roughly $100,000 in USDT, and a Telegram bot connected to the operation. Google also filed a civil lawsuit targeting the group’s infrastructure and said it is coordinating with AT&T, T-Mobile, and Verizon to block fraudulent messages before they reach subscribers. Google reported that Outsider Enterprise sent 2.5 million SMS messages to Android users during a two-week period in May, with Android users flagging 55,000 of them as fraudulent. In a separate phishing case, attackers impersonated InformData in an employment verification-themed email sent from verifications@international.informdata[.]com using SendGrid infrastructure. The message passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC despite the domain having a REJECT enforcement policy, because the sending path was authorized for international.informdata[.]com. The campaign illustrates that email authentication success does not necessarily indicate benign intent when legitimate or authorized infrastructure is abused. The employment verification lure observed by IRONSCALES used a Reply-To address at support@verifying[.]you, while its call-to-action directed recipients to verify.zippedscript[.]com. Both domains were described as attacker-controlled, creating a diversion between the authenticated sender identity and the infrastructure used for follow-up interaction. The campaign’s structure allowed the email to appear technically aligned while shifting recipient replies and clicks away from the impersonated brand domain. The call-to-action URL included a long per-recipient tracking token that could allow the attacker to correlate clicks with specific email addresses. IRONSCALES classified the message as high risk based on behavioral indicators including a Reply-To domain mismatch, a first-time sender pattern, and mismatched link destinations. The case underscores the continued use of human resources and employment verification workflows as credible business pretexts for phishing. Together, these reports show phishing operations operating at both industrial and targeted scales. Outsider Enterprise demonstrates the breadth of criminal phishing ecosystems built around mass URL generation, SMS delivery, and payment-card theft, while the InformData impersonation case shows how adversaries can exploit trusted email services and authentication alignment to increase the apparent legitimacy of business-themed lures.

item.title: Former Saydel School IT Employee Sentenced for Post-Employment Hacks
item.description: This digest window centers on an insider-threat case involving a former school district IT employee who retained access after leaving the organization and used it to disrupt systems over an extended period. The reporting highlights the operational impact of lingering administrative access in...
item.content:encoded: This digest window centers on an insider-threat case involving a former school district IT employee who retained access after leaving the organization and used it to disrupt systems over an extended period. The reporting highlights the operational impact of lingering administrative access in education environments, including disruption to identity, device management, learning platforms, and public-facing accounts. A former senior IT support specialist for Saydel Community School District, Ezekiel Dean Potter, was sentenced to 21 months in prison for cyberattacks against his former employer. Prosecutors said Potter retained credentials after his employment ended and targeted district systems over a 21-month period, causing disruptions across administrative, instructional, and communications platforms. According to court documents, Potter deleted the district’s Facebook page and disrupted access to educational platforms and accounts. He also deleted data from the district’s Apple School Manager account, which prevented staff from accessing the platform and disabled management of district MacBooks and iPads for roughly a week. The case also involved compromise of Google administrator accounts used to access the district’s Schoology learning management system. In January 2025, Potter used that access to delete employee Gmail accounts, disrupting teacher access and broader district operations. Potter was ordered to serve three years of supervised release following his prison term and to pay $59,668.81 in restitution to the Saydel Community School District and its insurer. The restitution reflects remediation costs associated with restoring affected services and addressing the damage caused by the unauthorized activity.

item.title: Velvet Ant Operation Highland Backdoored Critical Infrastructure Authentication for a Decade
item.description: This digest centers on a long-running Chinese-linked espionage operation targeting an isolated critical infrastructure network. Reporting from Sygnia describes how Velvet Ant sustained access for approximately 10 years by compromising authentication paths, modifying trusted Linux components, and...
item.content:encoded: This digest centers on a long-running Chinese-linked espionage operation targeting an isolated critical infrastructure network. Reporting from Sygnia describes how Velvet Ant sustained access for approximately 10 years by compromising authentication paths, modifying trusted Linux components, and creating covert remote execution routes into a segregated environment. The case highlights a mature persistence model focused less on noisy malware deployment and more on deep integration with identity, access, and network control planes. For technical defenders and executives alike, the incident illustrates how isolation alone can be undermined when authentication flows and administrative infrastructure are subverted. Sygnia attributed the campaign, dubbed Operation Highland, to the Chinese-linked Velvet Ant threat group. The activity reportedly affected a large organization’s isolated critical infrastructure network, where the attackers maintained espionage access for a decade. The operation began with compromise of internet-facing systems before the attackers pivoted inward using a modified GS-Netcat reverse shell and a custom SOCKS5 proxy. The campaign’s most significant feature was the abuse of authentication and application infrastructure to reach systems that were not directly exposed. Velvet Ant modified Nginx and FastCGI configurations to create a remote-execution pathway into the segregated environment through crafted HTTP requests. This allowed interaction with the isolated network without requiring a direct connection to the critical infrastructure segment. Sygnia also reported that the attackers replaced legitimate Linux PAM modules and OpenSSH components with trojanized versions. These backdoored components accepted hardcoded passwords, harvested credentials, captured commands, and preserved persistent access. By embedding themselves into core authentication mechanisms, the operators gained durable control over administrative pathways while blending into expected system behavior. The operation demonstrates a persistence strategy built around the compromise of trusted binaries and configuration layers rather than reliance on standalone implants alone. Backdoored PAM and OpenSSH components gave Velvet Ant access at the point where users and administrators authenticated, creating opportunities to collect credentials and observe operator activity. The use of modified web and FastCGI configurations further expanded the attackers’ ability to execute commands through infrastructure that would normally be considered part of legitimate service delivery. Remediation was described as complex because the compromised components were central to system authentication and remote administration. Sygnia noted that replacing many trojanized critical components could risk breaking authentication, locking out legitimate administrators, or causing operational outages. In the context of critical infrastructure, that operational fragility made eradication more difficult than simply removing a conventional malware payload. Overall, Operation Highland reflects an espionage pattern in which adversaries target the connective tissue between exposed enterprise systems and protected operational environments. The reported decade-long access underscores the strategic value of authentication infrastructure, reverse proxies, and administrative services as targets for stealthy persistence inside high-value networks.

item.title: Splunk CVE-2026-20253, Salesforce Phishing Abuse, and Anthropic Fable 5 Suspension
item.description: This coverage window is dominated by three distinct but operationally relevant developments: a critical unauthenticated Splunk Enterprise vulnerability, a phishing campaign abusing trusted Salesforce sending infrastructure, and a U.S. export-control action affecting access to Anthropic AI models....
item.content:encoded: This coverage window is dominated by three distinct but operationally relevant developments: a critical unauthenticated Splunk Enterprise vulnerability, a phishing campaign abusing trusted Salesforce sending infrastructure, and a U.S. export-control action affecting access to Anthropic AI models. Together, the reports highlight continued exposure in enterprise telemetry platforms, the limits of email authentication when legitimate SaaS infrastructure is abused, and growing regulatory pressure around advanced AI model access. Splunk released security updates for CVE-2026-20253, a critical Splunk Enterprise flaw rated CVSS 9.8. The vulnerability affects Splunk Enterprise versions below 10.2.4 and 10.0.7, while Splunk Enterprise 10.4 is not affected. Splunk Cloud is also not impacted because the affected PostgreSQL sidecar architecture is not used in that product. The flaw allows an unauthenticated user to create or truncate arbitrary files through a PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint. watchTowr Labs reported that the issue can be exploited through the /v1/postgres/recovery/backup and /v1/postgres/recovery/restore endpoints and can lead to pre-authenticated remote code execution on susceptible systems. Splunk Enterprise 10.0.0 through 10.0.6 are fixed in 10.0.7, and versions 10.2.0 through 10.2.3 are fixed in 10.2.4. The significance of the issue is amplified by Splunk’s common role in security monitoring, log aggregation, and incident response workflows. A pre-authentication code execution path in such a platform represents a high-value exposure because compromised monitoring infrastructure can affect both visibility and trust in downstream investigative data. IRONSCALES reported a credential-harvesting campaign that used a compromised or malicious Salesforce CRM organization to send an email disguised as a Medicare Transaction Facilitator attestation request. The message passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC with compauth=pass because Salesforce was an authorized sender for the outcomes.com domain. The email also used identical From and To addresses, aryan@outcomes.com, creating a self-send pattern through Salesforce. The campaign’s call-to-action linked to nexaroco.com/providerrelations/, which served an Office 365 credential-harvesting page. Themis initially flagged the message at 50% confidence, and additional phishing signals from the IRONSCALES community confirmed the classification. The activity illustrates how attackers can leverage trusted CRM infrastructure and valid domain authentication to reduce the effectiveness of controls that rely heavily on sender legitimacy. The reported lure also reflects a broader trend in phishing operations: attackers are increasingly aligning messages with business workflows that recipients may view as routine or compliance-driven. In this case, a healthcare- and Medicare-themed attestation request was paired with authenticated Salesforce delivery, increasing the appearance of legitimacy despite the credential theft destination. Anthropic suspended worldwide access to its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 AI models after receiving a U.S. government export-control directive ordering it to block access by foreign nationals. According to Anthropic, the directive applies to foreign nationals both inside and outside the United States, including the company’s own foreign-national employees. The company said this forced it to disable the models for all customers. Fable 5 had started rolling out on June 9 as a free limited-time offering for Pro, Max, and Enterprise customers through June 22 before being taken offline. Anthropic said new sessions would fall back to a user’s default model or Claude Opus 4.8, existing Fable 5 sessions would terminate with an error, and Platform requests to Fable 5 would fail. Anthropic disputed the rationale for the directive, stating that it appeared to stem from a narrow reported jailbreak involving a prompt to read a specific codebase and fix software flaws. The incident is notable for security teams tracking AI governance, model availability risk, and the intersection of software vulnerability analysis capabilities with export-control policy.
